name: Multiplication — Equal Groups and Arrays
whatIsIt: Multiplication is a fast way of adding the SAME number again and again. '4 × 6' means '4 groups of 6'. Instead of counting 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 slowly, multiplication jumps straight to 24. When we arrange the groups in neat rows, we get an ARRAY — rows and columns that show multiplication as a picture.
whyNeeded: Counting one by one is slow and tiring. When things come in EQUAL groups — boxes of laddus, rows of chairs, wheels on cycles — multiplication counts them all in one jump. It is the counting superpower for equal groups!
realLifeUses: Shopping: 5 packets of biscuits with 8 biscuits each — how many biscuits?
School assembly: 6 rows of children with 10 in each row
Cycles: 7 bicycles — how many wheels? 7 groups of 2!
Egg trays: 3 trays of 12 eggs for the party
Cricket: 4 overs bowled — how many balls? 4 groups of 6

vocabulary.meaning: Groups that all have the SAME number of things in them.
Counting equal groups in one jump: 4 × 6 means 4 groups of 6.
Things arranged in equal rows and columns — a multiplication picture.
The answer of a multiplication. The product of 4 × 6 is 24.

story.title: Four Baskets at the Mango Stall
story.text: At the mango stall, Anu sees 4 baskets, each holding 6 mangoes. 'How many mangoes altogether?' The seller smiles: 'Do not count all 24 one by one — the baskets are EQUAL groups. Four groups of six is 4 × 6. Jump-count by six: 6, 12, 18, 24. Twenty-four mangoes, in just four jumps!'
story.extractedProblem: There are 4 baskets with 6 mangoes in each basket. How many mangoes are there in total?
story.answerInStory: 4 groups of 6 makes 4 × 6 = 24 mangoes — counted in four jumps, not twenty-four!

visual.caption: Each dashed basket holds 6 mangoes. Four equal baskets: 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 = 4 × 6 = 24.

teachingGallery.title: Multiplication is equal groups
teachingGallery.note: So many groups, each the same size — count them all with a skip-count.

standardMethod.summary: Count the groups, count how many in each group, then multiply: groups × in-each = total.
steps: Check the groups are EQUAL — same number in every group.
Count the number of groups (4 baskets).
Count how many in ONE group (6 mangoes).
Write and solve the multiplication sentence: 4 × 6 = 24.

alternateMethods.name: Repeated addition
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When you are just starting, or want to check a multiplication.
steps: Write the group number once for every group: 6 + 6 + 6 + 6.
The total matches the multiplication: 4 × 6 = 24.

alternateMethods.name: Array method (rows and columns)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE multiplication and discover that 4 × 6 = 6 × 4.
steps: Arrange the objects in equal rows: 4 rows of 6.
Count a row (6) and count the rows (4): 4 × 6 = 24.
Turn your head sideways — now it is 6 rows of 4! Same dots, so 6 × 4 = 24 too.

alternateMethods.name: Skip counting
alternateMethods.whenToUse: For quick mental multiplication with friendly numbers.
steps: Say the table of the group size, one jump per group.
For 4 × 6: say 6, 12, 18, 24 — four jumps, done!

workedExamples.problem: A parking lot has 5 rows of cars with 3 cars in each row. How many cars are there?
steps: Groups are equal: 5 rows, 3 cars in each.
Multiplication sentence: 5 × 3.
Skip count by 3: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15.
There are 15 cars.

workedExamples.problem: Ravi says 4 × 6 = 10 because '4 + 6 = 10'. What went wrong?
steps: 4 × 6 does not mean 'put 4 and 6 together'.
It means 4 GROUPS of 6: 6 + 6 + 6 + 6.
Adding the two numbers mixes up + and ×.

trickPractice.intro: Multiplication is just skip-counting the same group again and again.

commonMistakes.mistake: Answering 4 × 6 with 10 by adding 4 + 6 instead of taking 4 groups of 6.
commonMistakes.fix: Read × as 'groups of'. 4 × 6 is 4 groups of 6 mangoes — picture Multi's baskets, then jump: 6, 12, 18, 24.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving the number of groups (4) or the group size (6) as the answer instead of the total.
commonMistakes.fix: The question asks for ALL the things together. After writing groups × in-each, finish the jump counting to the total.

commonMistakes.mistake: Multiplying when the groups are NOT equal (baskets of 6, 4, and 7 treated as 3 × 6).
commonMistakes.fix: Multiplication only works for EQUAL groups. If groups differ, add them one by one — or make them equal first.

easy.q: There are 3 plates with 4 laddus on each plate. How many laddus in total?
hintLadder: Are the groups equal? 3 plates, 4 on each — yes!

easy.q: Which sum is the SAME as 2 × 5?
hintLadder: Write 5 once for each group. How many 5s?

medium.q: A classroom has 6 rows of desks with 5 desks in each row. How many desks are there?
hintLadder: Rows and columns — this is an array!

medium.q: Multi arranged 24 mangoes in 4 rows of 6. Anu turned the tray sideways and saw 6 rows of 4. Who has more mangoes?

options.label: Anu, because 6 rows is more than 4 rows

hintLadder: Did any mango appear or disappear when the tray turned?
Count the total both ways: 4 jumps of 6, then 6 jumps of 4.
Same dots, same total — order does not change the product.
medium.explain: Turning an array swaps rows and columns but never changes the total: 4 × 6 = 6 × 4.

challenge.q: Three baskets hold 6, 4, and 7 mangoes. Ravi writes 3 × 6 = 18 mangoes. What is wrong?

options.label: The groups are not equal, so he cannot multiply — he must add 6 + 4 + 7

challenge.answer: The groups are not equal, so he cannot multiply — he must add 6 + 4 + 7
hintLadder: What must be true about the groups before you multiply?

challenge.q: A cricket match: a bowler bowls 4 overs, and each over has 6 balls. Another bowler bowls 24 balls. Who bowled more balls? Answer with the FIRST bowler's total.
hintLadder: First bowler: 4 overs of 6 balls — equal groups!
Now compare with the second bowler's 24.
challenge.explain: 4 × 6 = 24 — both bowled exactly the same number of balls!

hintLadder: Which number counts the groups? Which counts inside one group?

questions.q: There are 4 cycles in the shed. Each cycle has 2 wheels. How many wheels?
An egg tray array has 3 rows with 6 eggs in each row. How many eggs?
When is it OK to multiply to find a total?

options.label: Only when all the groups are equal
Whenever there are groups of any sizes

questions.answer: Only when all the groups are equal
hintLadder: Think of Multi's baskets — what was special about them?
Could Multi jump-count baskets of 6, 4, and 7?
Equal groups only. Different sizes must be added.

questions.q: 5 packets of biscuits, 8 biscuits in each packet. Total biscuits?

teachBackPrompt: Multi the Monkey wants YOU to teach a new monkey the jump-counting trick! Explain: what must be true about the groups, what 3 × 4 means, and show how to jump-count it. Use baskets, plates, or anything you like.

revisionCard.summary: Multiplication = equal groups counted in jumps. 4 × 6 = 4 groups of 6 = 6+6+6+6 = 24. Arrays show it in rows; turning the array proves 4 × 6 = 6 × 4. Rule: EQUAL groups only — unequal groups must be added.

gameMission.title: The Mango Basket Rush
gameMission.brief: Baskets keep arriving at Multi's stall! Jump-count each delivery before the timer mangoes roll away, and catch Ravi sneaking unequal baskets into the multiplication line.

realLifeProject: Set the table tonight! Count the plates, then the spoons per plate, and announce the total 'the monkey way': '4 plates × 2 spoons = 8 spoons!' Find two more equal-group multiplications hiding in your home (window grills, egg trays, chapati stacks) and write them down.

mentalMathMethod.name: Doubling to multiply
steps: ×2 is doubling.
×4 is double-double.
×8 is double-double-double.

abacusMethod.name: Repeated addition on the beads
abacusMethod.whenToUse: To multiply small numbers on the abacus.
steps: Add the group size once for each group.
Push up that many beads, that many times.
The final beads show the product.

visual.caption: Add 4 three times on the beads.

rememberIt.hook: Multiply = equal groups added fast. 4 x 3 = four groups of 3
rememberIt.unpack: 3+3+3+3 = 4 x 3 = 12. Rows x columns in an array. Order does not matter (4x3 = 3x4).

studentQuestions.a: Four groups of 3 (or 3 added four times) = 12. Multiplication is repeated equal-group adding.

studentQuestions.q: Is 4 x 3 the same as 3 x 4?
studentQuestions.a: Yes - both make 12. You can swap the numbers.
